coredata.dc:description: Rapid and accurate detection of Burkholderia gladioli (B. gladioli) and effective sterilization are crucial for ensuring food safety. Hence, a novel “loong frolic pearls” platform based on platinum-based fluorescent nanozymes (Pt-OCDs) and strand exchange amplification (SEA) was reported. Magnetic nanoparticles were modified on primer SEAF, while Pt-OCDs were covalently coupled with primer SEA-R. The highly efficient amplification capability of SEA permitted the accumulation of a large number of double-labeled amplicons. After magnetic adsorption, the supernatant was detected in reverse direction to collect colorimetric-fluorescence-photothermal signal values, enabling ultra-precise detection within 1 h. Furthermore, the Pt-based multifunctional nanoplatform generated abundant •OH and 1O2, which synergistically attacked B. gladioli and its biofilm, resulting in significant bactericidal efficacy within 30 min. This “triple-detection and double-sterilization” platform has been successfully applied in the field of food analysis with good recovery rates and immediate control over B. gladioli, thus demonstrating promising prospects for broad applications.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes:The measured signal value (y) was respectively put into the corresponding linear equation to obtain x (target bacterial concentration), and then the ratio between the measured target bacterial concentration and the actual added target bacterial concentration was multiplied by 100 % to obtain the recovery rate.

head.ce:title: Platinum-based fluorescent nanozyme-driven “loong frolic pearls” multifunctional nanoplatform for tri-mode ultrasensitive detection and synergistic sterilization of Burkholderia gladioli

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Engineering, Hefei University of Technology, Hefei 230009, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Tunxi Road 193, Hefei, Anhui province 230009, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid and accurate detection of Burkholderia gladioli (B. gladioli) and effective sterilization are crucial for ensuring food safety. Hence, a novel “loong frolic pearls” platform based on platinum-based fluorescent nanozymes (Pt-OCDs) and strand exchange amplification (SEA) was reported. Magnetic nanoparticles were modified on primer SEAF, while Pt-OCDs were covalently coupled with primer SEA-R. The highly efficient amplification capability of SEA permitted the accumulation of a large number of double-labeled amplicons. After magnetic adsorption, the supernatant was detected in reverse direction to collect colorimetric-fluorescence-photothermal signal values, enabling ultra-precise detection within 1 h. Furthermore, the Pt-based multifunctional nanoplatform generated abundant •OH and 1O2, which synergistically attacked B. gladioli and its biofilm, resulting in significant bactericidal efficacy within 30 min. This “triple-detection and double-sterilization” platform has been successfully applied in the field of food analysis with good recovery rates and immediate control over B. gladioli, thus demonstrating promising prospects for broad applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Burkholderia gladioli (B. gladioli) is a food-borne pathogen with a wide ecological range, classified as a Gram-negative bacterium associated with human infections (Becka et al., 2021; Moon et al., 2017). Dried mushrooms are one of the primary sources of B. gladioli contamination and are often polluted during the soaking process (Zhang, Guo, et al., 2023; Zhang, Han, et al., 2023). B. gladioli is capable of producing bongkrekic acid (BA), a respiratory toxin that can have severe consequences for human health. Severe BA poisoning can rapidly progress from liver and kidney injury to systemic multiple organ failure, resulting in high mortality rates (Kano et al., 2019; Shi et al., 2019). Over 9000 cases have been reported since the mid-20th century due to ingestion of food contaminated with B. gladioli (Anwar et al., 2017; Jiao et al., 2024). Additionally, biofilms act as protective barriers for bacteria and promote their long-term survival in food samples (Mendez et al., 2020). B. gladioli exhibits a strong biofilm-forming ability that is difficult to eliminate effectively through disinfection methods (Tseng et al., 2016). Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop an integrated platform for efficient detection and disinfection of B. gladioli to effectively prevent and control bacterial spread while ensuring food safety.

Currently, the most commonly employed method for B. gladioli detection is GB4789.29–2020, which involves routine microbial culture and requires at least one week to identify B. gladioli. However, several alternative techniques have been proposed to detect B. gladioli, including polymerase chain reaction (PCR), RPA-CRISPR/Cas12a, and loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) (Kuang et al., 2017; Li, Chen, et al., 2024; Zheng et al., 2023). These methods offer numerous advantages over conventional approaches in terms of speed, sensitivity, and specificity. Nevertheless, their application in field food safety monitoring is limited due to the requirement for multiple primer pairs and potential false positive results caused by residual contamination. Moreover, these methods exhibit poor reproducibility of results. In contrast, denaturing bubble-mediated strand exchange amplification method (SEA) represents a promising isothermal nucleic acid amplification technique that utilizes only a single pair of primers (C. Shi et al., 2016). This approach relies on the invasion of amplification primers into denaturation bubbles within dsDNA and employs Bst DNA polymerase for extension at the reaction temperature (Gao et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2024; C. Shi et al., 2016). Previously applied successfully to common pathogenic bacteria such as Vibrio parahaemolyticus, Staphylococcus aureus, and Listeria monocytogenes, SEA demonstrates excellent specificity and robust anti-interference capabilities (Sun et al., 2023; Zhang, Han, et al., 2023; M. Zhang et al., 2018). Therefore, the development of SEA-based detection methods offers significant potential for the rapid and straightforward field detection of B. gladioli. Currently, fluorescence and colorimetric-based sensors have been developed to rapidly detect various pathogenic bacteria (Li, Tang, et al., 2024; Pang et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024). However, the majority of these biosensors for B. gladioli detection employ a single signal strategy in the sample, which is susceptible to interference from the sample medium and may result in a false positive signal output. Moreover, numerous nanomaterial-based strategies depend on separate detection and disinfection forms of bacteria without effective countermeasures following a positive sensor test (Cao et al., 2024; Li, Fan, et al., 2024). Multifunctional nanozymes often exhibit diverse characteristics, including fluorescence, enzyme activity, and photothermal properties (Hou et al., 2024; X. Li et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2020). The characteristics of multifunctional nanozymes offer significant convenience for establishing an integrated platform for detection and sterilization. In light of the aforementioned considerations, there is an urgent need to develop an integrated platform using multifunctional nanozymes based on SEA isothermal amplification to enable multimodal detection of B. gladioli. The output channels of the multi-signal values are different and independent of each other, and mutual verification can be used as internal proofreading to greatly improve the accuracy of the detection results. Especially in the detection process of actual samples, because of the interference of sample matrix, single channel will easily lead to errors, so multi-channel verification is needed. Furthermore, the target bacteria in the sample can be eliminated in a timely manner after a positive result is obtained. This approach represents a novel strategy that is characterized by precision, speed, and environmental friendliness.

In this study, a novel platinum-based fluorescent nanozymes (Pt-OCDs) were synthesized to enable the inter-calibration of multiple signals. The introduction of magnetically driven SEA has further enhanced the sensitivity of the precision tri-modal sensing platform. More excitingly, Pt-OCDs can be further transformed after a positive result is determined. The developed Pt-OCDs grafted with Ce6 (Ce6@Pt-OCDs) can be composed of “big killers”, generating •OH and 1O2 mixing to synergistically eradicate B. gladioli and even biofilm (Fig. 1 ). OCDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method, and then Pt-OCDs were obtained by a simple chemical reduction method assisted by ultrasound to uniformly load Pt on OCDs. The platinum-based fluorescent nanozymes probe (one loong frolic orange bead) was generated by amidation and covalent coupling of rich in amino group (−NH2) chloroplatinic acid-doped orange carbon dots (Pt-OCDs) with the downstream primer SEA-R, which possesses carboxyl group (-COOH). This process resulted in the acquisition of colorimetric-fluorescence-photothermal triplet properties (Fig. 1A). Simultaneously, the magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) were modified on the upstream primer SEA-F to create a magnetic separation probe (another loong frolic black bead). In environmental samples containing B. gladioli, dynamic disintegration of the double-stranded DNA body occurred at a constant temperature, facilitating invasion by the double probes that is complementary to modified bubbles. The double-probe primers were extended from two opposite directions using the original duplex as a template with assistance from Bst DNA polymerase, thereby generating duplex amplification products containing both Pt-OCDs and MNPs markers. Magnetic adsorption was employed on the supernatant to determine signal value in the reverse direction while eliminating potential interference from magnetic materials. Colourimetric monitoring was achieved by employing peroxidase activity catalyzed by Pt-OCDs to convert colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue oxTMB through the action of H₂O₂. The use of a 660 nm laser to detect oxTMB revealed that the temperature signal is dependent on the concentration of bacteria, thus enabling the detection of field samples. The fluorescence signal intensity of Pt-OCDs in the supernatant exhibited a negative correlation with the concentration of the target bacteria. Furthermore, the samples were found to be positive, and the multifunctional platinum-based nanozymes platform was employed to eradicate the target bacteria. By loading chlorinated e6(Ce6) onto Pt-OCDs to achieve the modification of nanozymes, Ce6@Pt-OCDs can produce•OH and 1O2 to cover the bacterial surface and enhance the bactericidal efficiency (Fig. 1B). Consequently, as a novel approach, this multifunctional Pt-OCDs platform integrating “triple-detection and double-sterilization” can be applied flexibly in accordance with the specific situation, thereby reducing the consumption of unnecessary energy and reducing costs. Furthermore, this platform is capable of effectively eliminating bacteria and biofilm, preventing environmental contamination caused by B. gladioli in samples, which represents a significant advancement in pathogen monitoring.

Chloroplatinic acid, o-phenylenediamine, ferric chloride hexahydrate, sodium citrate, ethylene glycol, streptavidin, and sodium borohydride were all purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). 5 % Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA), Bst DNA polymerase, deoxyribose nucleoside triphosphate (10 mM), and 10 % of PEG-200 were purchased from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and phosphate buffered saline (1 × PBS, pH = 7.6) were all purchased by Sigma Chemical Company (St. Louis, Mo, USA). HAc-NaAc buffer was purchased from Yuanye Bio-Technology (Shanghai, China). Bradford Protein Assay Kit and Reactive Oxygen Species Assay Kit were purchased from Beyotime (Shanghai, China). SYTO 9/PI Live/Dead Bacterial Double Stain Kit was purchased from MKBio (Shanghai, China). Potato Dextrose Broth (PDB) medium and Luria-Bertani (LB) medium were purchased from Huankai (Guangdong, China).

The microbial species used in the experiments were Burkholderia gladioli (ATCC 33664), Listeria monocytogenes (ATCC 43251), Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 29213), Salmonella typhimurium (ATCC 29544), Escherichia coli (ATCC 25922), Cronobacter sakazakii (ATCC 12806), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (ATCC 15442), and Vibrio parahaemolyticus (ATCC 17802). B. gladioli and V. parahaemolyticus were incubated in PDB medium and LB medium (containing 3 % NaCl) overnight, respectively. The remaining strains were cultivated in LB medium. Before usage, broth cultures were centrifuged at 3000g for 15 min followed by removal of the medium and resuspension in sterile 1 × PBS. The bacterial samples will be diluted tenfold with PBS to achieve a concentration range of 100 to 108 CFU/mL.

21.6 mg of ferric chloride hexahydrate and 13.4 mg of sodium citrate were dissolved in 20 mL of ethylene glycol, followed by the gradual addition of 23.0 mg of sodium acetate and stirring for 30 min. The mixed solution was incubated in an oil bath at 220 °C for 10 h to obtain MNPs. An equal volume of activation mixture (containing 4 mM EDC and 2 mM NHS) was added to 100 μL of MNPs (10 mg/mL), and the -COOH was activated by incubation at room temperature for 30 min. Then, 5 μL streptavidin (SA, 5 mg/mL) was added and the reaction was carried out at room temperature with gentle agitation (20 ×g) for 3 h. Finally, residual active sites were blocked with 50 μL BSA (5 %) for 1 h, and MNS-SA was obtained by washing three times with 1 × PBS buffer (pH 7.6). SEA-F/MNPs-SA was prepared by incubating 20 μL MNPs-SA (500 μg/mL) with 15 μL SEA-F (10 μM) for 3 h at 4 °C for subsequent use.

The orange carbon dots (OCDs) were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method. Initially, 65 mM of phenylenediamine was dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water by sonication for 10 min. Subsequently, the resulting mixture was transferred to a sealed hydrothermal reaction vessel and allowed to react at 160 °C for 4 h, followed by gradual cooling to room temperature. To remove impurities, the reaction products were centrifuged at 10,000g for 15 min. The resulting supernatant was then filtered twice using a 0.22 μm filter. Finally, the filtrate was subjected to vacuum freeze drying for 24 h to obtain solid OCDs. The OCDs at a concentration of 1 mg/mL (5 mL) were thoroughly mixed with a solution of chloroplatinic acid at a concentration of 7.9 mM (1 mL). Subsequently, sodium borohydride solution at a concentration of 0.1 M (1 mL) was added and the mixture was sonicated for 3 h. The reaction products were then centrifuged at 10,000g for 30 min and the supernatant was discarded. The resulting precipitate was resuspended in deionized water (20 mL) and dialyzed for 24 h using a dialysis tube with a molecular weight cutoff of 3000 Da. Finally, the dialyzed solution was dried in a vacuum freeze dryer for 24 h to obtain solid Pt-OCDs. 20 mg of SH-PEG-NH2 was added to 1 mg/mL of Pt-OCDs (3 mL), stirred thoroughly for 2 h and stored at 4 °C. Then, 1 mg/mL of activated Ce6 (1 mL) was added and loaded onto the Pt-OCDs by amide reaction to obtain Ce6@Pt-OCDs. Ce6@Pt-OCDs was mainly used to improve the sterilization efficiency of the samples and inhibit the formation of biofilm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the properties of Pt-OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The 1 mg of Pt-OCDs was completely dissolved in 1 mL of ultrapure water, resulting in a 1 mg/mL solution of Pt-OCDs. Initially, to evaluate the fluorescence properties and determine the optimal excitation wavelength of Pt-OCDs, 200 μL of the Pt-OCDs solution was introduced into a cuvette and then analyzed using a fluorescence spectrometer (Lengguang technology Co., Shanghai, China). Then, the peroxidase activity of Pt-OCDs was evaluated using the TMB colorimetric method, and the Mitchell constant (Km) and maximum reaction rates (Vmax) were calculated based on the Mee equation (Pan et al., 2022). In further investigation, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Bruker, Magnettech, Germany) spectroscopy was used to observe the mixture of Pt-OCDs with H2O2 (with DMPO as a radical scavenger) to confirm •OH generation by nanozyme catalysis of Pt-OCDs. Furthermore, an investigation of the optimal catalytic conditions for Pt-OCDs nanozymes involved the study of their catalytic capacity at different pH values and temperatures. To verify the stability of Pt-OCDs, peroxidase activity and fluorescence were measured at 0,10,20, and 30 days. Specifically, a volume of 50 μL of Pt-OCDs was added to each of two polyethylene (PE) tubes. A solution of 1 mM H2O2 and 2.5 mM TMB was prepared with the addition of HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0), resulting in a final reaction volume of 500 μL.The incubation was conducted in a 40 °C shielded light metal bath for a period of 10 min. The absorbance of the reaction solution was observed and recorded at a wavelength of 653 nm. Another polyethylene tube containing 50 μL of Pt-OCDs was taken and 450 μL of PBS solution was added. Following 10 min incubation period at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity in the sample was measured and recorded. Each experiment was conducted in triplicate.

For the synthesis of SEA-R/Pt-OCDs, 15 μL of activator and incubated for 30 min at room temperature to activate the -COOH. Subsequently, 20 μL of Pt-OCDs was added to the mixed solution and further stirred at a rate of 200 ×g for 1 h to obtain SEA-R/Pt-OCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of conditions of tri-modal sensor platform
ce:section.ce:para: The four conditions of the tri-modal sensor platform were optimized, including the concentration of Pt-OCDs (25–175 μg/mL), concentration of primers (0.1–2.5 μM), incubation time (15–40 min) and temperature (52–67 °C). The 250 μL SEA reaction system consisted of 25 μL Bst buffer, 5 μL Bst DNA polymerase (8 U/mL), 5 μL deoxyribonucleoside triphosphate (10 mM), 25 μL 10 % PEG-200, 10 μL DNA (using DNA extraction kit for extraction), different concentration of double probe combination (different concentration of Pt-OCDs and primers), the remaining volume was filled with sterile water. The reaction solution was incubated in a thermostatic metal bath at diffenent temperature for 15–40 min, then placed on a magnetic rack for 2 min, and the supernatant was aspirated for use. 50 μL of the supernatant was added to 1 mM H2O2 and 2.5 mM TMB with HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0) to make the final reaction volume 500 μL. The incubation process was conducted in a 40 °C shielded light metal bath for a duration of 10 min. The absorbance of the reaction solution was monitored and documented. The oxTMB was then irradiated with a 660 nm laser for 180 s, and the resulting temperature change was recorded with a thermal imager. 50 μL of the supernatant was taken and 450 μL of the PBS solution was added. Following the 10-min incubation period at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity in the sample was quantified and documented. Each experiment was conducted in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The specific detection steps of tri-modal sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The 250 μL SEA reaction system consisted of 25 μL Bst buffer, 5 μL Bst DNA polymerase (8 U/mL), 5 μL deoxyribonucleoside triphosphate (10 mM), 25 μL 10 % PEG-200, and 70 μL double probe combination (35 μL SEA-F /MNPs-SA and 35 μL SEA-R /Pt-OCDs), 10 μL DNA (using DNA extraction kit for extraction), and 110 μL sterile water. The reaction solution was incubated in a thermostatic metal bath at 61 °C for 30 min, then placed on a magnetic rack for 2 min, and the supernatant was aspirated for use. 50 μL of the supernatant was added to each of two polyethylene (PE) tubes. 1 mM H2O2 and 2.5 mM TMB were added together with HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0) to make the final reaction volume 500 μL. The incubation was performed in 40 °C shielded light metal bath for 10 min. The absorbance of the reaction solution was observed and recorded. The oxidized TMB was taken and irradiated with a 660 nm laser for 180 s, and the temperature change was recorded with a thermal imager. Another PE tube containing 50 μL supernatant was taken and 450 μL PBS solution was added. After incubation at room temperature for 10 min, the level of fluorescence intensity in the sample was measured and recorded. Each experiment was repeated in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of B. gladioli by tri-modal sensor platform
ce:section.ce:para: The signal values of colorimetric-fluorescence-photothermal for the concentrations ranging from 100 to 109 CFU/mL of B. gladioli were determined following the test procedure outlined in section 2.8. A quantitative detection equation was established based on the logarithmic value of the concentration of B. gladioli and the detected colorimetric-fluorescence-photothermal signal values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity and stability of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To eliminate interference from common bacteria in complex environments, it is critical to validate the specific identification capability of the tri-modal sensor platform for B. gladioli. The platform was employed to analyze sample solutions containing 108 CFU/mL B. gladioli, L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, S. typhimurium, E. coli, C. sakazakii, P. aeruginosa, and V. parahaemolyticus. The colorimetric-fluorescence-photothermal signal values of each sample solution were recorded. In addition, to evaluate the stability of the sensor, sample solutions containing B. gladioli at concentrations of 103 and 105 CFU/mL were analyzed at different time intervals (0, 5, 10, and 15 d) and their respective output signal values were compared. All tests followed the procedure described in section 2.8.

For effective control of B. gladioli, bacteria at a concentration of 108 CFU/mL were subjected to different treatments including H2O, H2O2, Pt-OCDs, Pt-OCDs+H2O2, Pt-OCDs+H2O2 (660 nm laser), and Ce6@Pt-OCDs+H2O2 (660 nm laser). The bacterial residual rate was analyzed using the plate count method at a series of treatment times (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, and 90 min) to construct a time-lethal curve. In addition, the release of nucleic acid and protein by the bacteria was quantified to assess bacterial damage (Ashrafudoulla et al., 2020). Changes in bacterial surface morphology were observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU8020, Hitachi, Japan). Additional, the amount of reactive oxygen species (ROS) released and the ratio of live to dead bacteria were observed by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, LSM710, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibitory effect on the biofilm of B. gladioli
ce:section.ce:para: B. gladioli at a concentration of 108 CFU/mL was diluted to a ratio of 1:100 (v/v) in fresh PDB medium (200 μL) and transferred to the wells of 96-well polystyrene.

plates for incubation at 37 °C for 48 h. Crystal violet staining (CVS), SEM, and LCMS were used to evaluate the biofilm elimination effect of Pt-OCDs and Ce6@Pt-OCDs (Jiao et al., 2024; F. Liu et al., 2018; D. Zhang et al., 2024).

The Tremella fuciformis, Auricularia auricula, and Lentinula edodes used in this study were obtained from the vegetable market in Hefei, China. Firstly, we used the Chinese National Food Safety Standard GB4789.29–2020 and molecular identification techniques to detect the presence of B. gladioli in the samples (Jiao et al., 2024). Following the confirmation of the absence of B. gladioli in the samples, the target strains were added at concentrations of 103, 105, and 107 CFU/mL, respectively. The spiked edible mushrooms were homogenized using a homogenizer for 2 min followed by a resting period of 5 min to allow separation of the supernatant. This supernatant was then collected as the test solution. Additionally, to provide a more visual representation of the sensor's detection efficacy towards B. gladioli, bacterial counts were determined using plate counting method simultaneously. Furthermore, to evaluate the bactericidal effectiveness of Pt-OCDs and Ce6@Pt-OCDs against B. gladioli in real samples, an inoculum concentration of 108 CFU/mL was used for spiking into edible mushrooms.

SEA has gained acceptance as a rapid and straightforward isothermal detection method due to its independence from complex thermal cyclers and multiple temperature controls (C. Yan et al., 2020; S. Yan et al., 2024). B. gladioli is the sole known producer of BA, with BA production closely linked to the presence of the BA biosynthesis gene cluster bon (Anwar et al., 2017; Depoorter et al., 2016). Among the genes identified, bonJ has been identified as a core gene involved in BA biosynthesis, encoding an acyl transferase protein (Peng et al., 2021). Therefore, bonJ was selected as the target gene for SEA primer design aimed at detecting B. gladioli. The primer sequences were provided in Table S1. To validate the efficacy of the SEA primer pair for B. gladioli detection, samples with and without target DNA were subjected to gel electrophoresis analysis. As illustrated in Fig. S1, the results of agarose electrophoresis demonstrated the successful operation of the SEA amplification substrate in lane 1, which exhibited a series of bands upon the addition of the target DNA template. In contrast, lane 2 exhibited no amplification products other than a weak band corresponding to amplified primers when no target DNA template was present, confirming the absence of SEA product formation. Furthermore, the observed tandem repeat products can be attributed to self-priming extension induced by certain DNA polymerases employed in isothermal amplification systems (C. Shi et al., 2016). Consequently, it can be concluded that the utilized SEA primers effectively detect B. gladioli.

SA is a kind of four-polypeptide molecule with a molecular weight of approximately 60 kDa. SA has an extremely high affinity for biotin, which is one of the strongest noncovalent interactions known in nature (Liu et al., 2024). Therefore, to link SEA-F modified with biotin, SA was modified on the synthetic magnetic beads. As illustrated in Fig. S2 (A-B), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, Ltd., Japan) images demonstrate that a layer of SA was successfully modified on the surface of the MNPs. The MNPs-SA exhibited a uniform spherical shape with an average diameter of approximately 200 nm. In order to verify the magnetic absorption performance of MNPs modified with SA, the MNPs were placed on the magnetic frame for 2 min. During this period, the solution changed from turbid brown red to colorless transparent, as shown in Fig. S2 (C-E). To confirm the successful synthesis of SEA-F/MNPs, XPS full scans of MNPs, MNPS-SA, and SEA-F/MNPs, along with the corresponding characteristic peak spectra, are presented in Fig. S3. The electron binding energy positions of the Fe element are 710.81 eV and 724.26 eV, which correspond to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of Fe3+, respectively. This phenomenon is reflected in the three stages of the synthesis of SEA-F/MNPs (MNPs, MNPS-SA, and SEA-F/MNPs). Furthermore, as illustrated in Fig. S3E, reveals the presence of two distinctive O1s peaks at 530.87 eV and 532.9 eV, which are attributed to CO and CO, respectively. This indicates the existence of a considerable number of carboxyl groups in MNPs. SA contains a substantial number of amino groups, which can be amideated with the carboxyl group present in MNPs. As a result, the N-(C=O) characteristic peak at 399.49 eV can be observed in the N1s spectrum of MNPS-SA(Fig. S3I). Furthermore, the full spectrum of SEA-F/MNPs (Fig. S3C) demonstrated the presence of the P element, which also substantiated the successful binding of SEA-Bio to MNP-SA. Concurrently, the zeta potential also validated the successful synthesis of SEA-MNPs (Fig. S4).

The carbon dots (CDs) represent a novel class of carbon nanomaterials with quasi-spherical particles less than 10 nm in size. Compared to other semiconductors, CDs have attracted considerable attention due to their exceptional fluorescence properties, photostability, biocompatibility, high water solubility, and low toxicity. In addition, CDs possess abundant -COOH, -NH2, and other functional groups that provide active sites for surface modification (Ma et al., 2024; Thakur & Dan, 2021). Metal doping of the CDs enables the material to exhibit both intense fluorescence and peroxidase activity, facilitating the development of multifunctional nanozymes platform. Fig. 2A indicated that Pt-OCDs have a particle size distribution in the range of 10 nm. In addition, Fig. 2B showed that Pt-OCDs have lattice spacings of 0.228 nm, 0.195 nm, and 0.215 nm, corresponding to the Pt (111) crystal plane, Pt (200) crystal plane, and sp2 graphitic carbon's diffraction plane (102), respectively (A. Zhang et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2022). When OCDs were mixed with an aqueous solution containing chloroplatinic acid, followed by the addition of a reducing agent, stable liquid systems were formed, which subsequently led to the precipitation of Pt-OCDs from the solution phase. The ultra-small size of the OCDs provided an increased specific surface area that was conducive to uniform dispersion of numerous platinum (Pt). In addition, OCDs with abundant surface and edge defects generated additional active sites that ensured stability when loaded with high concentrations of Pt element. To ascertain the elemental composition with precision, energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analysis was conducted. Fig. 2(C-G) showed that the successful doping incorporation of elemental platinum into OCDs was conclusively confirmed. Complete X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) scans for Pt-OCDs along with characteristic peak spectra related to Pt, nitrogen (N), carbon (C) and oxygen (O) were shown in Fig. 2J.

Three valence states of Pt can be identified according to the doublet binding energy of Pt 4f7/2 and Pt 4f5/2 for as-prepared Pt-OCDs (Fig. S5A). Specifically, the electron binding energy positions of the Pt element were 70.94 eV and 74.23 eV corresponding to 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 for Pt0, 72.75 eV and 76.16 eV corresponding to 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 for Pt2+, and 75.01 eV and 78.34 eV corresponding to 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 for Pt4+, respectively. On further analysis, N1s can be divided into two characteristic peaks at 398.73 eV and 400.23 eV, attributed to CN and NH, indicating the presence of an abundant -NH2 in Pt-OCDs (Fig. S5B). At the same time, the positive value of zeta potential measurement of Pt-OCDs also proved the enrichment of -NH2 (Fig. 2K). C1s has three separate good characteristic peak, they attributed to the following carbon functional groups: CC (284.8 eV), C- O/C-N (286.51 eV) and CO (288.17 eV) (Fig. S5C). O1s can be divided into three characteristic peaks at 530.50 eV, 531.71 eV and 532.9 eV, which are attributed to PtO, CO and CO, respectively, indicating that Pt is successfully doted in the OCDs (Fig. S5D). In addition, in the X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Germany) pattern of Pt-OCDs (Fig. 2K), the crystal planes of Pt metal Pt (111), Pt (200), Pt (220), and Pt (311) have diffraction peaks at 39.8°, 46.2°, 67.8°, and 81.6°, respectively. It is shown that the Pt catalyst has a face-centered cubic crystal structure (D. Pan et al., 2018), which is consistent with the crystalline structure of the TEM image. All these provided further evidence for the doping of Pt element in OCDs.

The fluorescence emission intensity of Pt-OCDs was measured at an excitation wavelength range of 390–480 nm to explore their characteristics, and it was determined that the optimal excitation wavelength was 440 nm (Fig. S6(A-B)). To investigate the catalytic mechanism of H2O2 by Pt-OCDs, EPR spectroscopy was employed, which revealed a signal peak intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 (Fig. 2M), confirming •OH as the primary radical generated during the catalytic process (Chen et al., 2020). Furthermore, when H2O2 and TMB were introduced as substrates in the presence of catalysts H2O, OCDs and Pt-OCDs individually, only Pt-OCDs exhibited peroxidase activity by converting colorless TMB to blue oxTMB product (Fig. S7(A-B)). The affinity between Pt-OCDs and substrate was further investigated using the Km as a parameter. As shown in Fig. S8(A-D), the Km values of Pt-OCDs were determined to be 0.839 mM (using TMB as the substrate) and 0.136 mM (using H2O2 as the substrate), based on the analysis of the double reciprocal curve. Notably, Pt-OCDs exhibited significantly lower Km values compared to previously developed nanozymes, indicating higher peroxidase activity (L. Gao et al., 2021). Temperature and pH were identified as critical factors influencing the catalytic activity of Pt-OCDs. Firstly, high catalytic activity was observed within a temperature range of 5 to 55 °C with maximum efficiency at 40 °C (Fig. S9(A-B)). Then, enhanced peroxidase activity of Pt-OCDs was observed under acidic conditions with pH of 4.0 in HAc-NaAc buffer solution (Fig. S9(CD)). Additionally, both the fluorescence properties and the peroxidase activity of Pt-OCDs remained stable over a period of 30 d (Fig. S10(A-B)).

In this study, the concentration of Pt-OCDs, the concentration of the double probes (SEA-F/MNPs and SEA-R/Pt-OCDs), the incubation time, and the incubation temperature were systematically investigated and optimized to achieve optimal detection performance. Initially, the formation of the SEA-R/Pt-OCDs probe was achieved by incubating different concentrations of Pt-OCDs with SEA-R. As shown in Fig. 3 (A-C), stable colorimetric-fluorescence-photocaloric signal values of the amplified product were observed when the concentration of Pt-OCDs ranged from 25 to 100 μg/mL. However, when the concentration exceeded 100 μg/mL, all three output signal values showed a significant increase.The observed phenomenon suggests that the excess Pt-OCDs is not bound to SEA-R, does not participate in the amplification process, and remains present in the supernatant. Therefore, Pt-OCDs at a concentration of 100 μg/mL was used for subsequent experiments. Then, the concentrations of double probes were optimized. The signal values exhibited a declining trend with increasing double probes concentration, until the concentration surpassed 1.5 μM, at which point the signal stabilized (Fig. 3(DF)). Under identical reaction conditions, the amplification products obtained by adding more than 1.5 μM of double probe primers would reach saturation due to the limitation of enzyme activity and the availability of different substrates. Therefore, there is no need to use excessive primers to avoid wastage. In addition, the amplification time (15–40 min) was optimized as shown in Fig. 3(G-I). The three signal values showed a decreasing trend with increasing amplification time, and after reaching an amplification time of 35 min, the signal values tended towards stability. This indicates that at this particular time, the amplification products obtained had reached a saturation level. Finally, as shown in Fig. 3(J-L), the amplification temperature was optimized within a range of 52–67 °C. Notably, the optimal detection signals was obtained at 61 °C, which is well within the recommended temperature range for Bst DNA polymerase activity. This indicates that the amplification temperature is closely related to the activity of Bst DNA polymerase.

The feasibility of the integrated platform was evaluated following the optimal conditions previously established. Additionally, a variety of conditions were created to assess the efficacy of the tri-modal sensor platform with regard to signal processing. As shown in Fig. 3(M-O) and Fig. S11(A-C), group 1 contained the target DNA template, and the gel electrophoresis bands of the amplification products were bright. The colorimetric-fluorescence-photothermal signal values of the supernatant after magnetic separation tended to be stable. It indicated that the amplification was stable, and there was almost no amplification product in the supernatant. Group 2 was a negative control without a target DNA template, which was opposite to group 1, proving that background interference could be almost ignored. Group 3 was incubated at ambient temperature for 30 min, and the gel electrophoresis results revealed an absence of bands. Additionally, the supernatant exhibited significantly higher signal values compared to group 1, indicating the absence of self-ligation and non-specific amplification of double probes under ambient temperature conditions. Group 4 was primer SEA-R without connecting Pt-OCDs and gel electrophoresis showed normal positive bands after amplification, while the colorimetric-fluorescence-photothermal signal values could not be detected. This phenomenon shows from the side that the three signal values are all the results obtained due to the successful connection of primer SEA-R to Pt-OCDs. In addition, Fig. 2K showed that the zeta potential of Pt-OCDs linked to primer SEA-R changes from positive to negative, which also proved the successful design of the SEA-R/Pt-OCDs probe. In group 5, primer SEA-F was not connected to MNPs. The gel electrophoresis of amplification products showed that whether the connection of MNPs or not did not affect the progress of SEA amplification. However, the colorimetric-fluorescence-photothermal signal values of group 5 were much higher than those of group a, indicating that Pt-OCDs was non-magnetic. These also demonstrate the importance of the simultaneous presence of double probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear analysis and specificity study of tri-modal sensor platform of B. gladioli
ce:section.ce:para: The DNA template was added to the SEA reaction system and subsequently subjected to amplification. After magnetic absorption of the amplified product, the supernatant was collected for the determination of colorimetric-fluorescence-photothermal signal values using a tri-modal sensor platform specific to B. gladioli (Fig. 4A). The concentration of the target bacteria was determined by detecting Pt-OCDs in the supernatant. In the event that the target is present, both the amplification product and the Pt-OCDs are attracted to the magnet simultaneously. The more targets, the more amplification products, and the less free SEA-R/Pt-OCDs in the supernatant, the lower the signal value detected. Conversely, in the absence of a target, amplification is not possible and all SEA-R/Pt-OCDs are present in the supernatant, resulting in the highest possible signal value being detected. First, H2O2 and TMB were added to the supernatant, and the amount of H2O2 catalyzed was inversely proportional to the concentration of target bacteria. The absorbance of the blue product, oxTMB, at 653 nm exhibited a gradual decrease with increasing concentration of the B. gladioli (Fig. 4(BC)), which was visually corroborated by the observed color change (Fig. 4H). Meanwhile, the absorbance value and the concentration of B. gladioli had a good linear relationship between 102 and 108 CFU/mL, and the linear equation was y (absorbance) = −0.13425 × Lg(CFU/mL) + 1.15007, and the linear correlation coefficient R2 was 0.99111 (Fig. 4D). The limit of detection (LOD) of the colorimetric assay was 13.67 CFU/mL. The results were calculated by 3σ/slope, where σ represents the standard deviation of the detection values from 10 measurements of a blank sample (Tang et al., 2024). Similarly, a direct correlation was observed between the signal intensity in the fluorescence detection mode and the concentration of the target bacteria, whereby an increase in bacterial concentration resulted in a decrease in signal intensity (Fig. 4(E-F)), because higher bacterial concentration resulted in more extracted DNA templates and more amplification products, and less Pt-OCDs in the supernatant after magnetic aspiration. As illustrated in Fig. 4G, when the concentration of B. gladioli was in the range of 102–108 CFU/mL, the linear relationship equation was y (normalized FL intensity) = −0.13778 × Lg(CFU/mL) + 1.04605, with a linear correlation coefficient R2 of 0.99347. The LOD using the fluorescence assay was 15.55 CFU/mL. Photothermal mode detection is the temperature change produced by illuminating the blue product oxTMB with a 660 nm laser for 180 s. The bluer the colorimetric product, the higher the temperature after irradiation. The photothermal results can further verify the accuracy of the test results. Fig. 4(H-J) demonstrated a negative correlation between the change in temperature signal and bacterial concentration. When the bacterial concentration was 102–108 CFU/mL, the linear relationship equation was y(temperature) = −2.82008 × Lg(CFU/mL) + 41.07007, R2 was 0.98355. The LOD of the photothermal detection platform was 19.98 CFU/mL. The tri-modal sensor platform which we developed exhibits superior sensitivity and a wider linear range compared to other reported B. gladioli detection platforms (Table S2). Furthermore, given the presence of other bacteria in the sample during actual testing, test specificity is critical to avoid interference from common bacteria. Fig. 4(K-M) and Fig. S12(A-C) showed that except for the target bacterium B. gladioli, and no statistically significant differences were observed for other common bacteria compared to the control group (Fig. S11(A-C)). Therefore, it can be concluded that the pair of primers SEA-F and SEA-R identified in this study is specific for B. gladioli and selectively detects it in an environment containing various pathogenic bacteria. In addition, the tri-modal sensor showed excellent stability with no significant change in detection over a period of 15 d (Fig. 4(NP)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of B. gladioli and biofilm by platinum-based nanozyme platform
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the sterilization efficacy and mechanism of a Pt-based multifunctional nanozyme platform were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5B, when B. gladioli at a concentration of 108 CFU/mL was exposed to H2O (group a), H2O2 (group b), and Pt-OCDs (group c) for 0–90 min, no significant changes in bacterial counts were observed. The low concentration of H2O2 exhibited mild sterilizing ability, while Pt-OCDs itself did not possess bacteriostatic ability. However, in group d (Pt-OCDs+H2O2 without 660 nm laser) and group e (Pt-OCDs+H2O2 with 660 nm laser), the target bacteria concentration decreased to 103 CFU/mL within 70 min. This reduction can be attributed to the catalytic effect of Pt-OCDs on H2O2 production, generating a large amount of •OH, which is lethal to target bacteria. ·OH cannot directly penetrate the cell membrane (Salvador, 2011), so it mainly acts on the cell surface, and reacts with structural components of the cell membrane or cell wall surface to oxidize. To achieve faster and more thorough elimination of B. gladioli, Ce6 was successfully loaded on Pt-OCDS as depicted in Fig. 5A and confirmed by Fig. S13 and Fig. S14(A-F). In group f (Ce6@Pt-OCDs+H2O2 with a 660 nm laser), complete inactivation of B. gladioli at a concentration of 108 CFU/mL was achieved within only 30 min due to the generation of •OH along with 1O2 as demonstrated by the results in (Fig. 5B). Ce6@Pt-OCDs+H2O2 significantly improved the elimination efficiency. 1O2 is the excited state of oxygen molecules after absorbing energy, and its outermost layer has an empty orbital, which is easy to obtain electrons and combine with other substances to form stable chemical bonds. 1O2 mainly attacks various amino acids and ribobases to cause irreversible oxidative damage to proteins and DNA (RNA) (Di Mascio et al., 2019). ·OH and 1O2, as the main members of ROS, act synergistically to cause strong oxidative stress in bacteria, and their functional substances will be attacked and damaged to varying degrees. Finally, the purpose of eliminating bacteria in a short time is quickly achieved. Furthermore, the effect of different conditions on the membrane permeability of B. gladioli was evaluated by measuring extracellular UV-absorbing substances and extracellular protein content. It was found that groups d, e, and exhibited significantly higher membrane permeability compared to the control group a (Fig. 5(CD)). The increased cell membrane damage led to the release of macromolecules such as nucleic acid, resulting in the impairment of normal metabolic activity. Overall, the results of this study provide valuable insights into the sterilization efficacy, mechanisms and improved elimination strategies using the Pt-based multifunctional nanozyme platform against B. gladioli. At the same time, proteins are important intracellular components for maintaining bacterial metabolism and physiology. The Pt-based fluorescent nanozyme platform in this study disrupts proteins or disrupts intracellular proteins released from the cell membrane to induce death of B. gladioli. As shown in Fig. 5E, under plate counting and CLSM, it can be seen more intuitively that the number of inactivated bacteria increased significantly in groups d and e, while no live bacteria remained in group f. This killing ability is closely related to the level of ROS. The microstructure of B. gladioli was observed by SEM. Cells in control group a had complete morphology, smooth and regular surface, and uniform cell size and distribution. There was little difference between groups b and c and the control group. In groups d and e, some of the cells showed irregular folds on the outer surface and a certain degree of rupture accompanied by leakage of intracellular substances, and some of the cells were deformed. In group f, the bacterial cell membrane was severely collapsed with extensive leakage, and most of the bacteria lost their complete structure, resulting in a complete loss of bacterial activity (Fig. 5E). The results observed by SEM were consistent with those obtained by plate counting and CLSM as described above.

Biofilm formation not only enhances the resistance of pathogens to environmental stress, but also increases the risk of persistent infection and cross-contamination (Hossain et al., 2021). B. gladioli has a strong ability to form biofilms, which may contribute to the survival of these microorganisms in the environment and host (Jiao et al., 2024). As shown in Fig. 5F, the biofilm in group a reached a mature stage with dense layers and no apparent cell rupture. Conversely, groups d and e exhibited significantly reduced biofilm density and a more dispersed appearance. Group f struggled to maintain complete biofilm integrity, resulting in a relatively higher number of planktonic bacteria. When stained with the fluorescent nucleic acid dyes syto9 and PI, live cells appeared green while dead cells were red. Treatment with Pt-based fluorescent nanozymes resulted in significant changes in the structure of the biofilm, characterized by disorder, looseness, increased cell death rates, and an abundance of red dead cells within the field of view. The SEM observations and laser confocal results corroborated those obtained from the CVS analysis (Fig. 5G). In conclusion, the Pt-based fluorescent nanozyme platform effectively controls B. gladioli.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and disinfection performance in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Edible mushrooms, especially dried ones, are susceptible to contamination by B. gladioli during the soaking process. To evaluate the detection capability of the proposed tri-modal sensor platform on real samples, we selected soaked Tremella fuciformis, Auricularia auricula, and Lentinula edodes as target samples for analysis. Each sample was artificially spiked with B. gladioli at concentrations of 103, 105, and 107 CFU/mL and quantified using the developed tri-modal sensor system. The recoveries obtained with this tri-modal sensor platform ranged from 91 % to 114 % with a standard deviation of less than 8.5 %. These results demonstrate both the applicability and accuracy of our strategy in the analysis of real samples (Table 1 ). Furthermore, the comparison between the plate count method (PDA medium) and our tri-modal sensor platform showed no significant difference in detection values (Fig. S12), confirming the accuracy of our developed sensor system and its potential for practical applications in sample analysis. In addition, after validating the applicability of our detection platform on real samples, we also evaluated its sterilization efficiency on these samples in this study. When a real sample was spiked with B. gladioli at a concentration of 108 CFU/mL followed by treatment with different reagent combinations for 30 min, no significant differences were observed between group b treated with H2O2 or group c treated with Pt-OCDs compared to control group a without any treatment. However, when H2O2 + Pt-OCDs were added either with or without a 660 nm laser (group d vs. group e), no significant difference was observed between them, but both showed reduced B. gladioli levels down to107 CFU/mL compared to the control group a. After 30 min of treatment with H2O2 + Ce6@Pt-OCDs in group f, the B. gladioli concentration in the samples was significantly reduced, demonstrating an apparent bactericidal effect (Fig. 5H). However, the bactericidal effect observed in the samples is generally not as potent as that achieved with pure reagents, which could be attributed to the protective environment created by the samples themselves. Other bacterial species were also present in the sample, resulting in a lower percentage of B. gladioli killed than before. Overall, this integrated platform has significant potential for detection and control in real samples.

Overall, a specific detection platform for the tri-modal of B. gladioli under isothermal reaction was established using the novel nanozyme Pt-OCDs, combined with primers of SEA to form “loong frolic pearls”. We employ an inverse strategy to determine the signal value of the supernatant, which eliminates interference from magnetic nanomaterials compared to other sandwich structure-based detection strategies. Our developed colorimetric-fluorescence-photothermal tri-modal sensor platform based on SEA isothermal amplification achieves rapid results within 1 h while demonstrating excellent specificity. In addition, the mutual validation provided by the tri-modal signal values serves as an internal proofreading and enhances the accuracy of the detection results. The LOD for the platforms was determined to be 13.67 cfu/mL (colorimetric), 15.55 cfu/mL (fluorescence), and 19.98 cfu/mL (photothermal), respectively, with direct linear relationships within the bacterial concentration range of 102–108 CFU/mL. Notably, the use of the double probes sensor system for detection in edible mushroom samples also provided superior tolerance and accurate results. Moreover, Pt-OCDs modified for the first time (Ce6@Pt-OCDs) can effectively eradicated target bacteria and prevented biofilm formation, providing new insights into the prevention of secondary contamination caused by pathogenic bacteria and ensuring compliance with environmental and food safety standards. Furthermore, the tri-modal sensor can also be used to develop qualitative and quantitative double probes commercial kits, and combined with smart detection devices (such as smartphones) to create identification databases to standardize the detection process and improve detection efficiency.
Not required.

This work was supported by National Key Research and Development program, China [2022YFF1100702]; Guangdong Provincial Key Research and Development Project [2022B1111040002]; the Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation [2208085J11]; the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JZ2022HGTB0271).

Yingwang Ye: Funding acquisition, Supervision.
